paper_title: In silico and in vivo analyses of pollen aperture pattern formation

methods.description: Implementation of the Gierer-Meinhardt model in FlexPDE to simulate aperture patterning in Arabidopsis pollen grains in both 1D and 3D domains.

steps.description: We implemented the coupled GM equations in FlexPDE, a finite-element partial differential equation (PDE) solving environment.
steps.searchable_summary: Implement GM equations in FlexPDE for PDE solving

steps.description: A domain was modeled with a size that corresponded to the size of wild-type haploid (1n) pollen grains with an observed front-view surface area averaging 550 μm².
steps.searchable_summary: Model domain size for 1n pollen grains

steps.description: We explored parameter values that determine the model's kinetics on the 1D WT domain to find values that would produce three equally spaced spikes.
steps.searchable_summary: Explore parameter values for 1D WT domain kinetics

steps.description: We verified that these parameter values satisfy the conditions for Turing-type patterning.
We tested a range of parameter values to determine how much change our model could tolerate while still producing Turing patterns.
steps.searchable_summary: Test parameter range for Turing pattern tolerance

steps.description: We increased the domain to match the larger size of the diploid (2n) Arabidopsis pollen grains.
steps.searchable_summary: Increase domain size for 2n pollen grains

steps.description: We tested if a change of the same magnitude in the opposite direction would also change the number of spikes produced by our model.
steps.searchable_summary: Test domain size reduction effect on spike number

steps.description: We started out with the same parameter values as in 1D to test the model in 3D.

methods.description: Analysis of how changes in model parameters affect the number of spikes in the aperture patterning of Arabidopsis pollen grains.

steps.description: We performed a one-parameter-at-a-time analysis, varying the model parameters in both the WT and the larger-size domains.
We observed that varying the diffusion rates of the two morphogens changed the number of spikes our model was able to produce.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze effect of diffusion rate on spike number

steps.description: We found that varying the decay rates of the two morphogens changed the number of spikes produced.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze effect of decay rate on spike number

methods.description: A forward genetic screen on an EMS-mutagenized population of Arabidopsis plants to identify mutants with novel pollen aperture phenotypes.

steps.description: We performed a forward genetic screen on an EMS-mutagenized population of Arabidopsis plants.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct forward genetic screen on EMS-mutagenized Arabidopsis

steps.description: We found mutants belonging to two complementation groups, macaron (mcr) and doughnut (dnt), with novel pollen aperture phenotypes.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify mutants with novel pollen aperture phenotypes
